🇮🇪 Ireland’s Timeless Heritage Top 10 Places That Define the Emerald Isle
From ancient stone castles perched on green hills to dramatic cliffs carved by the Atlantic, mythical landscapes, and vibrant towns steeped in centuries of history this video is a love letter to Ireland’s soul.

I turned the most breathtaking spots into short clips and wove them into one journey through the very best of Irish heritage, 
1.  Cliffs of Moher
2.  Dublin
3.  Tipperary
4.  Cork
5.  Giant’s Causeway
6.  Galway
7.  Killarney National Park
8.  Dingle Peninsula
9.  Kilkenny

kept.text:
What people imagine life expectancy in 1900 looked like:

- Everyone dropping dead at 32 in the street
- Nobody alive past 40
- Grandparents a mythical concept
- A society of children and corpses
- Heart disease, diabetes, and cancer waiting politely for someone to live long enough to develop them
- The whole population a living advertisement for seed oil and statins

What 1900 actually looked like:

- Life expectancy at birth: 47. Dragged down by infant mortality.
- Survive to age 5, you'd reach 65 to 75 comfortably
- Reach 21, your odds of seeing 70 matched a person in 1990
- Queen Victoria died that year aged 81. Meat, suet, brandy.
- Florence Nightingale: 90. Tolstoy: 82. Carnegie and Bismarck: 83.
- None of them on statins. None of them avoiding butter.
- The killers of the under-40s: tuberculosis, pneumonia, dysentery, typhoid, scarlet fever, smallpox, childbirth.
- Not heart disease. Not type 2 diabetes. Not obesity-related cancer.
- Heart attack so rare in 1900 the first textbook on coronary thrombosis came out in 1912 and described it as a curiosity

And yet there is an entire class of commentator, podcast host, and blue-tick public intellectual who will look at "life expectancy was 47 in 1900" and conclude, with great confidence, that everyone was dropping dead of butter at thirty. They have a degree. They have a Substack. They have not, at any point, opened the appendix of the data they are quoting.

The number was an average pulled down by babies dying of cholera. The adults were fine. They lived into their seventies on lard and walked everywhere. Misreading this in 2026, with the data one Google search away, isn't a mistake. It's a tell.
